Episode of non-cardiac chest pain 18 months ago, evaluated in emergency department with negative cardiac workup
Childhood strabismus surgery (bilateral medial rectus recession) at age 7, with good alignment postoperatively
No history of seizures, stroke, or demyelinating disease

Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis, diagnosed at age 34
No family history of epilepsy or brain tumors

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. Mildly anxious affect. Speech fluent without dysarthria. Follows complex commands. Recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No evidence of cognitive impairment on bedside testing.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN I: not tested. CN II: visual acuity 20/20 OU with correction. Visual fields full to confrontation. Funduscopic exam normal bilaterally, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: intermittent convergent eye deviation noted, inconsistently present during examination; at times eyes track smoothly in all directions without restriction. During sustained lateral gaze testing, patient intermittently develops convergence spasm with pupillary constriction and accommodative effort. Bell reflex present and normal bilaterally. CN V: sensation intact V1-V3 bilaterally. CN VII: symmetric face. CN VIII: hearing grossly intact. CN IX/X: palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone normal in all extremities. Bulk normal. Strength 5/5 throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities with consistent effort. No drift on pronator testing. No fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. Midline splitting of vibration sense noted on forehead testing (non-anatomic pattern).
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal base, normal stride length, tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Convergence spasm observed intermittently during extraocular movement testing: episodes of bilateral eye convergence with miosis, followed by periods of entirely normal ocular motility. The pattern is inconsistent with a fixed cranial nerve palsy. Positive finding of midline vibratory splitting on forehead examination. Ophthalmology consultation notes comitant esotropia measured at 25-30 prism diopters during episodes, but alignment normalizes spontaneously. Hess red-green testing during an episode showed inward displacement of both eyes with equal field sizes, but testing repeated 20 minutes later was entirely normal.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden-onset double vision when looking at distant objects, worsening over 3 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: A 29-year-old right-handed woman presents to the neurology inpatient service after referral from ophthalmology for evaluation of acute-onset horizontal diplopia. The patient reports that approximately 3 weeks ago, she began noticing double vision when looking at distant objects, particularly when watching television or looking across a room. The diplopia is described as horizontal, with images side by side, and resolves completely when she covers either eye. She notes the symptoms are intermittent and seem to worsen during periods of stress or fatigue, and she has noticed improvement on weekends when she is more relaxed. Near vision activities such as reading on her phone or computer do not provoke diplopia. She denies any associated headache, nausea, vomiting, or change in vision clarity. There is no history of recent head trauma, febrile illness, or new medications. She reports increased daily screen time over the past several months due to remote work, averaging 10 to 12 hours per day on digital devices. The patient was initially evaluated by ophthalmology, who documented intermittent comitant esotropia of 25 to 30 prism diopters at distance and variable measurements at near, with full ocular ductions and no nystagmus. The ophthalmologist noted that the esotropia was inconsistently present during different portions of the examination, with periods of normal alignment. Given her childhood history of strabismus surgery and the acute presentation, she was referred to neurology to exclude intracranial pathology. The patient reports significant psychosocial stress over the past year, including a difficult divorce, workplace instability, and estrangement from a family member. She endorses poor sleep quality with early morning awakenings and intermittent episodes of palpitations and chest tightness over the past several months. She has a maternal aunt diagnosed with multiple sclerosis, which has been causing her considerable anxiety about her own neurological health.

background.sleep: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes

eeg.impression: Normal EEG. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing. Normal background activity with symmetric posterior dominant rhythm. Intermittent muscle artifact noted in bilateral temporal regions, likely volitional jaw clenching. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; no clinical events captured during this session.

Normal appearance of cranial nerves III, IV, and VI bilaterally
Normal cerebral blood flow patterns on perfusion-weighted imaging
mri.impression: Essentially normal brain MRI. No evidence of demyelinating disease, mass lesion, stroke, or brainstem pathology. Normal cranial nerve anatomy. Clinical correlation recommended.

background.sleep: Normal sleep architecture observed during overnight recording

output.impression: Prolonged 48-hour video-EEG monitoring. One clinical event captured (diplopia with convergent eye deviation) with NO EEG correlate. Normal EEG throughout entire recording, including during the clinical episode. No epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Only one clinical event captured during the monitoring period.

additional_observations: Normal cervical spinal cord signal and caliber
Normal thoracic spinal cord without signal abnormality
No evidence of demyelinating plaques in the spinal cord
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelination, or structural abnormality. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: functional diplopia convergence spasm functional neurological disorder

results.title: Convergence spasm: a functional or organic disorder?
results.summary: Convergence spasm (also termed spasm of the near reflex) is characterized by intermittent episodes of convergence, miosis, and accommodative spasm. While historically attributed to organic posterior fossa lesions, the majority of cases in modern series are functional in nature. Key distinguishing features include intermittency, variability during examination, and resolution with monocular viewing. The triad of convergence, miosis, and accommodation during episodes is pathognomonic.

results.title: Functional visual symptoms in neurology: a practical approach
results.summary: Functional visual symptoms including diplopia, visual field deficits, and convergence spasm are increasingly recognized as part of the FND spectrum. Diagnosis should be made on positive clinical signs (inconsistency, incongruity with anatomical patterns) rather than by exclusion. Convergence spasm producing intermittent esotropia can be identified by the accompanying miosis and accommodative effort, which distinguishes it from a true sixth nerve palsy.

results.title: Acute acquired comitant esotropia: differential diagnosis and management
results.summary: Acute acquired comitant esotropia (AACE) has multiple etiologies including digital device overuse, decompensated phoria, intracranial pathology, and functional causes. Convergence spasm should be considered when esotropia is intermittent, variable, and associated with miosis. MRI of the brain is recommended in all cases of AACE to exclude posterior fossa lesions, particularly in pediatric patients.

output.summary: Literature supports that convergence spasm presenting as intermittent comitant esotropia is most commonly functional in modern case series, though posterior fossa lesions must be excluded with MRI. The hallmark diagnostic triad is convergence, miosis, and accommodative spasm during episodes. Diagnosis relies on positive signs of inconsistency and incongruity rather than exclusion alone.

interactions: Sertraline + Sumatriptan: Potential risk of serotonin syndrome when combining SSRIs with triptans. Risk is generally low at standard doses but patients should be counseled on symptoms (agitation, tremor, hyperthermia).
warnings: SSRIs including sertraline can rarely cause mydriasis; however, there is no established association between sertraline and convergence spasm or esotropia
Abrupt discontinuation of sertraline may worsen anxiety and depressive symptoms
alternatives: If SSRI-related visual side effects suspected: consider switching to duloxetine (SNRI) or buspirone (non-serotonergic anxiolytic)
CBT should be considered as primary or adjunctive treatment for anxiety/depression

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with functional diplopia due to convergence spasm

differential.diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder with convergence spasm
differential.key_features: Intermittent, variable esotropia with miosis during episodes (convergence spasm triad); normal ocular motility between episodes; midline vibratory splitting (positive functional sign); significant psychosocial stressors; normal MRI excluding structural cause
Excessive screen time (10-12 hours daily), acute onset esotropia; however, the intermittency, associated miosis, and variability during exam are more consistent with convergence spasm than fixed AACE

differential.diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis (internuclear ophthalmoplegia or sixth nerve palsy)
differential.key_features: Family history of MS in maternal aunt, single nonspecific white matter focus on MRI; however, comitant esotropia pattern, normal VEP, no dissemination in space or time, and normal spinal MRI argue strongly against
Diplopia and variable ocular findings could suggest myasthenia; however, AChR antibodies negative, repetitive nerve stimulation normal, no ptosis or fatigable weakness
MRI brain shows no brainstem pathology, no mass lesion, normal cranial nerve anatomy

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with and without contrast to exclude intracranial pathology, particularly brainstem lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with incidental nonspecific white matter focus; no brainstem lesion, no demyelination pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for any epileptiform activity or focal dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, ANA, AChR antibodies to rule out metabolic, autoimmune, and neuromuscular junction causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except mildly elevated TSH; AChR antibodies negative

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture a clinical event and correlate with EEG
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical event captured with no EEG correlate, supporting functional etiology

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on convergence spasm and functional diplopia to inform differential diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Literature confirms convergence spasm is most commonly functional, diagnosed by triad of convergence, miosis, and accommodative spasm

optimal_actions.action: Request formal psychiatric evaluation to assess comorbid anxiety and depression
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate depression (PHQ-9: 14) and severe anxiety (GAD-7: 16) with significant psychosocial stressors

optimal_actions.action: Formulate diagnosis of functional neurological disorder based on positive clinical signs (convergence spasm triad, midline vibratory splitting, inconsistency) rather than exclusion alone
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diagnosis supported by positive signs and consistent clinical picture

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to exclude brainstem or posterior fossa pathology before attributing diplopia to functional cause
Recognize convergence spasm (convergence + miosis + accommodative effort) as a positive sign of functional visual disorder
Identify midline vibratory splitting as a positive functional neurological sign
Diagnose FND based on positive clinical signs, not merely by exclusion of organic disease
Assess for comorbid psychiatric conditions (anxiety, depression) and psychosocial stressors
Communicate diagnosis to patient in a supportive, validating manner that acknowledges symptom reality
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing brainstem stroke or mass based on the nonspecific white matter focus without clinical correlation
Initiating anticoagulation or thrombolysis in the absence of stroke evidence
Starting antiepileptic drugs without EEG evidence of epileptiform activity
Performing unnecessary invasive procedures (lumbar puncture, brain biopsy) when clinical picture and MRI are reassuring
Dismissing symptoms as 'not real' or 'all in your head' — FND symptoms are genuine neurological symptoms
Diagnosing myasthenia gravis and starting pyridostigmine based on variable diplopia alone without confirmatory testing
key_reasoning_points: Acute acquired comitant esotropia requires MRI to exclude intracranial pathology, particularly brainstem lesions — this was appropriately performed and was normal

Convergence spasm (spasm of the near reflex) is identified by the triad of convergent eye deviation, pupillary miosis, and accommodative effort during episodes, distinguishing it from a true sixth nerve palsy
The intermittent and variable nature of the esotropia during examination, with periods of normal alignment, is inconsistent with a fixed cranial nerve palsy or structural lesion
Midline splitting of vibratory sense on forehead testing is a positive functional neurological sign (vibration should not split at midline as the frontal bone is a single structure)
The single nonspecific 3 mm white matter focus is an incidental finding that does not meet McDonald criteria for MS and should not drive the diagnosis toward demyelination
Mildly elevated TSH represents subclinical hypothyroidism — a red herring that cannot explain acute diplopia or convergence spasm
Family history of MS in a relative raises patient anxiety but does not significantly increase pretest probability when MRI and VEP are normal
FND should be diagnosed based on positive clinical signs (inconsistency, incongruity with recognized neurological disease) rather than solely by exclusion of organic disease
High screen time and prior strabismus surgery provide plausible alternative explanations that must be weighed but do not fully explain the convergence spasm pattern with miosis
Comorbid moderate depression and severe anxiety are commonly associated with FND and support the diagnosis

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to multiple confounding factors: childhood strabismus history suggesting recurrence, high screen time suggesting digital device-related AACE, incidental white matter lesion raising MS concern, mildly elevated TSH as metabolic red herring, and family history of MS increasing patient and clinician anxiety. The convergence spasm pattern with miosis and midline vibratory splitting provide positive functional signs, but require careful neuro-ophthalmological reasoning to identify.
